South Africa → USA · USRAP §207 · Day 1 to Year 5

Landing Pad

Every step one family ran, in order, with the timings that actually happened, from landing day to the 5-year mark. Tap a step, expand it, tick it when done.
Guideline only: built from previous families' real experiences. Every case is different, agencies, amounts, timings, and rules vary and change, always confirm against your own case documents and your agency's answers in writing.
0 of 17 activities done · 0%
1Briefing 2Documents 3Medicals 4Medicaid 5Apartment 6Match Grant 7Support 8Food 9Driving 10School 11Budget 12Flight Loan 13Rights 14Grievance 15Daily life 16Protect 17Year 1-5
1

CWS briefing

Land day 1, rest · briefing day 2✓▶

The day you land, you do one thing: rest. The agency normally takes you straight to a hotel or temporary housing, that night is for sleep, not paperwork. The briefing at your resettlement agency (CWS or whichever agency holds your case) happens the day after: caseworker assigned, 90-day clock context set, week 1 schedule laid out.

⚠ Landing-day rule: sign NOTHING on the day you land, above all not a lease. Landing day is hotel-and-rest day, that is the normal process, so any housing paper appearing at the airport or the hotel on day one is already out of sequence. You will be exhausted, jet-lagged, and signing in a legal system you have known for hours. The answer, politely and by email: "Thank you, we will review and sign tomorrow." One night changes nothing for the agency and everything for you, the lease is the single document that follows you longest (see the Apartment milestone).
  • Get your caseworker's name, email, and phone in writing on day one.
  • Ask what is scheduled for you in week 1 and 2, write the dates down yourself.
  • Keep every paper they hand you. One folder. It all gets asked for again.
From here on, anything you need: ask by EMAIL only. Verbal requests evaporate, emails create a record and unlock programs they will not volunteer. Be specific, ask early.
📎 Source documents for this meeting: passports · I-94 printouts · the US admission approval letter (the Department of State letter with the family photos, your §207 proof, valid one year from issue).
2

Documentation

6 documents, roughly in this order✓▶
1

I-94 papers

Week 1▶

Your I-94 is the arrival record that proves status. Retrieve one per family member, including children.

  • Printable at i94.cbp.dhs.gov, or your agency pulls it with you.
  • Check every field: name spelling, date of birth, Class RE (refugee), Admit Until D/S.
  • Your passport CBP stamp + I-94 prove refugee status and work authorization immediately, before any card arrives. You can do I-9 onboarding with these.
📎 Sources: passports · the US admission approval letter. The I-94 printout you produce here joins the source pack for every appointment after this one.
2

Social Security numbers

Week 1 · card ~2 weeks later▶

Apply at the SSA office for every family member, adults and children. Bring passports + I-94s.

  • You leave with a receipt letter, the physical card takes about 2 weeks in the post.
  • The receipt is usable in the meantime for several applications.
  • When cards arrive: do not carry them. Safe place at home. Replacements are capped at 3 per year, 10 per lifetime.
📎 Bring: passports · I-94s · US admission approval letter. ⚠ After the application wave is done, the Social Security card LIVES AT HOME in a safe place, never in a wallet, same for passports, the admission letter, and all originals. Carry photos and copies, fetch originals only when an office demands them.
3

EAD work permit cards

Auto-triggered on arrival · ~2 weeks▶

The Employment Authorization Document is triggered automatically when you arrive, no separate application. Cards are mailed to your agency, ours took 2 weeks.

  • Category A03 (refugee). Check the validity dates and diarise the expiry.
  • The card says not valid for re-entry: do not leave the US before green card without advance permission.
  • Remember you could already work from day 1 on the I-94 + stamp, the card just makes onboarding smoother.
📎 When the EAD cards arrive: photograph front and back, add them to the source pack, file the originals at home. The EAD becomes a primary ID for the bank, Florida ID, and employers.
4

Internet

Move-in week · doubles as proof of address▶

We used Xfinity: uncapped internet with a free extra cell line for about $40/month. Shop the intro deals, they change monthly.

Your first Xfinity invoice is a proof-of-address document, you will use it for the Florida ID. Order internet early so the invoice exists by week 3.
5

Bank account

Same week the lease is signed▶

Open the account as soon as the lease is signed.

  • What we used: lease as address proof, social security as ID number, passport as identity.
  • No social yet? Try applying with the I-94, some branches accept it, then add the SSN later.
  • While you are there: ask about a secured credit card, the US credit score clock only starts when you open something that reports.

Start the credit score, day one of banking

  • Secured credit card: you deposit a small amount (often $200 to $500), that deposit becomes your limit, and the card reports to the credit bureaus. Use it for one small recurring expense, pay the FULL balance every month, never miss. A "prepaid card" that does NOT report to bureaus builds nothing, ask the banker specifically: "does this card report to Experian, Equifax and TransUnion?" Only yes counts.
  • Keep usage under ~30% of the limit, under 10% is better. The score rewards low usage + perfect payment history + account age, so open it early and keep it open.
  • Make the lease work for the score: ask the landlord or complex office whether rent payments are reported to the bureaus (many use services like Bilt, RentTrack, or Esusu). If yes, 12 months of on-time rent becomes 12 months of credit history for free. If not, Experian Boost (free, experian.com/boost) adds your phone, internet, and utility payments to your Experian file, no subscription needed.
  • Skip paid credit-builder subscriptions for now, the free stack (secured card + Boost + reported rent) covers it. First FICO score appears about 6 months after the first account starts reporting.
📎 Bring: passport · SSN card · signed lease · EAD card. I-94 printout as fallback if the EAD has not arrived.
6

Florida ID

Week 3 · they are pedantic▶

State photo ID at the DHSMV office, we did ours in week 3. They are very pedantic with documents, bring everything:

  • Passport
  • EAD and/or social security card
  • Two proofs of address: lease + first Xfinity invoice worked for us. Bank statements work after a month. FPL bill also counts.
  • I-94, they ask for it.
You walk out with a temporary paper card, the plastic card posts later. Children can get IDs too, useful for school and flights.
📎 Bring: passport · EAD card · SSN card · I-94 · TWO proofs of address (lease + a utility bill, bank letter, or internet invoice). Second address proof is the one families most often forget.
3

Medicals, the health screen

Booked by agency · weeks 2 to 5✓▶

The refugee health assessment at the county health department. What actually happens:

  • Blood test maps your immune system, the results decide which vaccines each person needs after.
  • Urine test done on site.
  • Stool sample kit goes home with you, you complete it and bring it back.

Bring passports, I-94s, all vaccination records from SA, and Medicaid cards if they have arrived.

Families get back-to-back slots at one site. Plan it as one trip, one morning.
📎 Bring: passports · I-94s · vaccination records · Medicaid plan cards (whichever plan DCF assigned, common ones in Florida are Molina, Sunshine, Simply) · US admission approval letter.
4

Medicaid · medical + dental plan

Apply week 1 to 2 · approved ~2 weeks✓▶

Register for Medicaid through DCF (your caseworker assists). Ours approved in about 2 weeks.

  • In Florida you are assigned a managed-care medical plan (the state uses several, e.g. Molina, Sunshine, Simply Healthcare) and a dental plan (DentaQuest runs Florida Medicaid dental statewide).
  • You receive: gold Florida Medicaid ID cards + your medical plan's member cards + DentaQuest dental cards, per person.
  • Register on your medical plan's member portal (for Molina that is MyMolina.com), pick a primary care doctor, and note the 24-hour nurse line on the card.
Keep your home address current with DCF, not just for post. The address on file decides which plan, and which county, you are covered in (see the warning below).
Heads up, coverage change: a 2025 federal law ends Medicaid/CHIP for refugees from 1 October 2026 (cover limited mainly to green-card holders after that), and it is in flux / being challenged in court. Plan ahead: file your green card (I-485) as early as allowed, ask your caseworker about RMA and sliding-scale community health centers, and see the Safety net tab. Confirm your own status with your caseworker.
⚠The county trap: your plan can be registered in the wrong county▶

What happens: when your agency sets up your Medicaid, the medical plan is often registered under the agency’s office address, not your home address. If the agency office is in one county (for example Miami-Dade) and you are placed in another (for example Broward), Florida auto-assigns a managed care plan for the agency’s county. That plan (for example Molina) may not cover the doctors and clinics where you actually live.

The tell: you hold a Medicaid card, but a local clinic says you are “not covered” or “out of area,” and you are charged out of pocket for a routine visit. That charge is the wrong-county plan showing up as cash.

Fix it:

  • 1. Update your home address with Florida Medicaid eligibility (DCF / MyACCESS) to where you actually live. The eligibility address drives the plan region.
  • 2. A move to a new region is a state-approved “for cause” reason to change plans outside open enrollment, you do not have to wait.
  • 3. Call Choice Counseling (the Medicaid enrollment broker) on 1-877-711-3662, or use flmedicaidmanagedcare.com / the FL Medicaid Member Portal, and pick a plan that operates in your county.
  • 4. Ask the new plan for “continuity of care” so recent visits can be reprocessed, then ask the clinic to hold and re-bill. Charges already paid may be refunded.
Dental is usually fine: DentaQuest is statewide, so this trap is specifically the medical managed-care plan, not dental.
What the fix looks like in practice: once the region is corrected, the broker moves you onto a plan that actually serves your county, and the new plan starts on the 1st of the following month, managed-care switches take effect at the next month boundary, not the same day. So a clinic visit in the gap before the new plan starts is usually NOT covered retroactively, keep those receipts and either pursue a back-dated claim or treat them as self-pay. From the switch date forward you are on a county-correct plan and the out-of-area charges stop. Florida Medicaid plans that serve Broward include Simply Healthcare and Sunshine Health, among others.
📎 Bring: passports · I-94s · SSN receipts · US admission approval letter · lease or other address proof once you have it.
5

The apartment, push back

Week 1 to 2 · the biggest decision✓▶

The apartment is the single most important placement decision, everything else hangs off the address: school zone, IDs, bank, filings.

  • Agencies may propose a 1-bedroom for a family with a child. Push back. Ask, politely and by email, for something reasonable for your family size.
  • Ask to be placed near other refugee families, the day-to-day support of neighbours who walked the same road is worth more than an extra 10 minutes of commute.
  • Before signing: get pet permission, condition issues (mould, water damage), and the deposit figure in writing. Verbal confirmations do not count.
  • Never sign on arrival day or in the room. Take the lease away, sleep on it, read it fresh the next morning, then sign. Any landlord or caseworker who insists it must be signed immediately is telling you something about the document.
  • ⚠ Non-negotiable: an early-cancellation clause in the lease. Make sure the lease lets you terminate early, typically exercisable after about 3 months, for a fixed once-off fee, ours was about $100. Why it matters: your first job offer may be in another city or state, and a 12-month lease with no exit clause either kills the offer or costs you months of double rent. Ask for the clause BEFORE signing, confirm the fee amount and the notice period in writing, and keep that page in your folder. If the landlord refuses any exit clause, raise it with your caseworker, this is exactly the kind of term to push back on.
  • Photograph everything on move-in day.
Most of your PIR budget (see 💰 tab) flows to this lease as deposit + rent. You rarely see the cash, but it is your money being spent, ask for the running expenditure.
Tone: always friendly and respectful, but know your rights and push back where it matters, and the lease is where it matters most. After every discussion about the apartment, send a same-day recap email. Verbal promises about units, pets, repairs, or deposits are worth exactly nothing. See step 17, Know your rights.
📎 The signed lease is itself a source document from day one: file the original with the early-cancellation page flagged, photograph every page, it is your proof of address for the bank, Florida ID, school, and every filing.
6

Matching Grant

Intro, then the 30-day registration✓▶
1

Matching Grant introduction

Week 1 to 2 · listen, decide later▶

Your agency will introduce the Matching Grant (MG) employment program. This session is the introduction, registration is a separate step (step 9) with a hard 30-day deadline from arrival.

  • MG is one of several options (others: refugee cash assistance, TANF routes). MG worked for us: cash + transport stipend + job placement support, in exchange for employment commitments.
  • Ask in this meeting exactly what the monthly amounts are in writing, and what triggers reductions.
  • Understand the no-double-dip rule: MG replaces other cash assistance, you cannot draw both.
2

Matching Grant registration

⏰ BEFORE day 30 from arrival▶

The actual MG sign-up, week 2 for us. There are other options, but MG worked for our family.

  • Hard deadline: register before 30 days from arrival. Diarise it the day you land.
  • Both adults sign. Keep a copy of every form, and note the total amounts and term stated on them.
  • Send paystubs to your caseworker every time income changes, the program is graded on closing your case positively (self-sufficient).

MG deep dive · what the signed agreement actually gives you · 14 tabs from a real case

Figures below are from one real signed 2026 CWS agreement for a 2-adult + 1-child case, all personal data stripped. Your amounts scale with case size, the mechanics are the same.

1The headline numbers▶
Monthly cash allowance$440/mo (2A+1C table figure)
Cash windowenrollment to day 180 or self-sufficiency, whichever first
Housing + essential utilitiesagency-paid until your income covers them or day 240
Food + transport for job searchcovered during the program, separate vouchers
Program endday 240, everything on you after, employed or not

The cash table scales by case composition. Ask for the Monthly Cash Allowance table at pre-enrollment, it is a standard form.

2The single most important sentence▶

"The agency will ensure my housing and essential utilities are paid until I earn enough income, am terminated from the program, or day 240, whichever is earliest."

Translation: your rent is not your problem while MG runs, even after the initial resettlement money is spent. The thing to fear is not month-2 rent, it is the day-240 cliff. Work backwards from it: a job by month 4-5 gives you 3+ months of paychecks and a buffer before the cliff.

3The clock▶
  • Day 0: eligibility date (your arrival)
  • Day ≤30: enrollment, you sign the agreement pack
  • Day 180: cash allowance ends + 180-day report filed
  • Day 240: program ends, housing support ends, full self-sufficiency assumed

Calculate all four dates yourself from your arrival date on day one, and write them where you will see them.

4What you sign, the 6-document pack▶
  • MG Agreement, the contract: cash amount, housing commitment, your obligations
  • Eligibility & Assessment, employability per adult, barriers (health conditions get written here, answer honestly, it protects you on job matching)
  • DCF Referral, instructs the state to process SNAP + Medicaid ONLY, no state cash (taking state cash terminates MG)
  • RAP placement registration, mandatory per adult
  • Authorization to Release Information, lets agencies coordinate your file
  • CRS Referral, registers you with the local Comprehensive Refugee Services office, your nearest physical help desk, save the address and number
5Your 12 obligations▶

Summarised from the agreement: do your best to secure a job by day 240 · participate in English training and your Self-Sufficiency Plan · keep regular contact with the office · attend and arrive on time for ALL arranged interviews, including entry-level · accept the first appropriate job offered (prior experience does not exempt you) · once employed, work hard and follow employer policies · provide first pay stub + employer details · report job difficulties · inform the office before resigning · keep the job until at least day 240 unless agreed · do NOT access state cash assistance during the program · maintain residence in the service area and notify every change (name, address, phone, employment).

6The compliance folder, start day one▶

Bring to the office as they arrive: all DCF letters · Medicaid cards · immunization cards · children's school enrollment · proof of adult English class enrollment · driver's license copy · first paycheck copy · employer details (company, supervisor, address, phone) · receipts for everything donated to you · car registration if gifted one · volunteer trip sheets.

This file is audited at the 180 and 240-day reviews. One folder, everything in, from the first week.

7Cash steps down as you earn▶

The allowance reduces as income rises, by design. It is a bridge, not a wage. Budget as if the cash ends at day 180 (it does) and the housing ends at day 240 (it does).

8The first disbursement, real numbers▶

Three separate signed vouchers, one payment, the day after enrollment (real 2A+1C case):

Cash Assistance$440
Food Assistance$785
Public Transportation$140
Total month 1$1,365

The "monthly cash allowance" is only the cash line, food and transport arrive as separate vouchers on top. Photograph every voucher before handing it back, and ask whether food and transport recur monthly or were front-loaded. With housing agency-paid, month-1 support was far stronger than any pre-arrival model.

9The employment machine▶

At enrollment you are assigned a named MG Job Developer, a different person from your caseworker. The cadence is binding: report every two weeks, attend every referral, give advance notice with justification for absences. In return the Job Developer helps with applications and resume, provides referrals and directions, accompanies you to your first interview, and files bi-weekly progress reports.

Get the Job Developer's name and direct contact in writing on day one, and verify the name printed on your form, in our real case the printed name was wrong and corrected by hand.

10The tax-counseling pack▶

Expect to sign: a US Federal Tax Compliance form (duty to file, consequences, benefits of regular W-2 work over cash/gig jobs), a W-4 walkthrough, a sample paycheck explaining withholding, and a gig-economy warning, Uber/DoorDash income is untaxed at source, you owe it yourself.

VITA, the IRS Volunteer Income Tax Assistance program, gives free tax-return help from IRS-certified volunteers (generally for incomes under ~$67k, exactly this phase), and TCE does the same for age 60+. Find the nearest free site with the official IRS locator: irs.gov free tax preparation programs. Use it for your first filing, bring the source-document pack plus every pay stub and the W-2.

11The exit is pre-written, read it day one▶

The Case Closure Letter template is in the pack before the program even runs. At day 240 it certifies you Economically Self-Sufficient or not, and either way unlocks 5 further years: CRS community services (5 yrs) and Legal Services (5 yrs), explicitly including the green card application (I-485) at your 1-year anniversary, plus the ongoing duty to file AR-11 within 10 days of every move. The program ends, the relationship does not.

12Printed dates, verify everything▶

In one real pack: an orientation dated "01-00-1900", an assistance end-date matching neither day 180 nor 240, and a closure letter printing an eligibility date a month off the real one. All template noise, none malicious, any could bite at an audit. Check every printed date against your own arrival-date arithmetic before signing, and query mismatches at the first budget review.

13Your rights at the budget reviews▶

The program runs mandatory reviews (enrollment, post-employment, day 180, day 240). At any of them, and at any time in between, you may request in writing: the Monthly Cash Allowance table for your case size, your MG budget worksheet showing the housing and utility costs being carried, and an itemised record of every disbursement made on your case. Same one-thread-per-issue email habit as step 17, polite, written, with a receipt-confirmation request.

14If you lose the job, the 5-year safety net▶

MG ending does not mean the program is done with you. Retrenched, laid off, or the job collapses at month 10? The net underneath, in order:

  • Refugee employment services run for 5 YEARS from arrival (60 months), not 240 days: re-placement, job preparation, On-the-Job Training, re-credentialing and recertification, and career-laddering for professionals. Walk back into your CRS office (the same one from the enrollment pack) and ask to be re-opened for employment services, that is exactly what the 5-year window exists for.
  • "Our agency has no funds for credential conversion" is not the end of the road. Re-credentialing, credential evaluation, recertification and navigating state licensing are funded through a separate ORR channel, Florida DCF Refugee Services, not out of your resettlement agency's R&P or Matching Grant budget. A "no funds" answer only means it is not in that pot, the money for it sits in the DCF Refugee Services / employment-services stream. Go directly to your CRS or DCF Refugee Services office and ask for credential evaluation and recertification help, plus the career-laddering, vocational training and English classes for professionals. Eligible up to 60 months (5 years) from arrival or date of asylum.
  • CareerSource runs a dedicated Refugee Employment Program for anyone in the country under 5 years with legal status: placement, employed-worker upskilling contracts, OJT, credentialing of prior education. Free, walk in.
  • Within the first 12 months only: Refugee Cash Assistance (RCA) and Refugee Medical Assistance can bridge a no-income gap for households not eligible for TANF/Medicaid. After month 12 this door closes permanently.
  • Once you have US work history: state Reemployment Assistance (unemployment insurance) is an earned benefit, apply the week the job ends, it is not charity and does not affect immigration status.
  • Day one of any job loss: email your CRS office the same day (one thread, step 17 habit), register at CareerSource that week, and keep every termination document, the 5-year services need proof of status + arrival date, your source-document pack covers it.

Mark your own 5-year date (arrival + 60 months) somewhere permanent. Every refugee-specific door closes on that date.

15How the job expectation really works, and 4 myths▶

This is the part that throws SA families most, because we plan ahead and over-analyse, and that tips into panic. Read it once, calmly. Resettlement is a bridge to self-sufficiency, not a push off a cliff. The agency has federal targets to get you employed and onto that path, so "take a job soon" is genuinely their goal, not a trick.

  • You are expected to job-hunt yourself and take a job fast, even entry-level (warehouse or packing, retail, the big-box stores) if you have not landed your own first. You speak English and are usually over-qualified, that is your advantage, use it. If you do not find your own within their window, the agency will present jobs and you are expected to accept one. Treat it as a stepping stone, not the destination.
  • Myth 1, "my first salary must cover all my rent." No. The agency does NOT expect your whole first paycheck to go to rent. While you are employed but not yet earning enough, they keep covering rent, or a portion of it, plus utilities. You can even ask to put your own salary toward another basic need first, like a vehicle, while they still carry the basics.
  • Myth 2, "if the job will not cover rent, turn it down." No. Do not refuse jobs on a salary-versus-rent sum. Take the "good enough for now" job, stay employed, keep hunting for better. Benefits continue as long as you are Employed or Studying, that is exactly what the agreement says.
  • Myth 3, "they will find me a professional job." They are not equipped to, and do not have to, find you better than entry-level work. Laddering up to your field is on you, that is what the 5-year employment services in the previous tab exist for.
  • Myth 4, "they can just cut me off or evict me if I do not jump." Housing is a basic need and you cannot be evicted without a lengthy legal process. Some offices use pressure or scare lines ("we will stop your rent", "there is no programme", "your money is finished") to rush you. Anchor on what is PUT IN WRITING BY EMAIL, not what is said out loud or by text. If something is real, it will be in writing. If it is not in writing, treat it as pressure, not policy.
The flip: get employed soon, stay employed, and communicate and negotiate your budget and needs in writing with the office. Employed plus communicating is the combination that keeps the support flowing. Panic and silence is what breaks it.
Community wisdom, not legal advice. Programmes vary by agency and state, confirm specifics against your own signed agreement and what your office puts in writing.
📎 Bring: passports · I-94s · SSN cards or receipts · EAD cards if arrived · lease · US admission approval letter.
7

Support and volunteers

Ongoing · lean on it✓▶
💛 To the volunteers, thank you

The furniture, the food, the lifts, the forms, all of it mattered. But what carried us was something no program line item covers: you treated us like family. You sat with us, asked how we were really doing, celebrated the small wins, and made a strange country feel like somewhere we belonged from the first week. The physical support got us settled. The emotional support got us through. We will not forget it, and we will pass it on to the next family who lands.

From a family who arrived with suitcases and was met with open arms.

Accept the help, it is given gladly, and one day pay it forward to the next family.

  • Connect with church and community volunteer networks through your agency.
  • Join SARAH, South African Refugee Assistance & Help groups on Facebook for accurate, current program detail from people inside the process.
  • Dial 9-1-1 for any life-threatening emergency: police, fire, ambulance. Free from any phone, even with no plan or SIM, works in every US state. Teach it to the children in week one.
  • Dial 2-1-1 (Broward/Miami helpline) for everything urgent but NOT life-threatening: food, rent, crisis, and family services referrals, free, 24/7.
  • Alma, the IRC's free WhatsApp assistant: save +1 619-658-5100 in WhatsApp, type "hello". Answers questions 24/7 on public benefits, employment, financial literacy and housing, your rights, documents, and local services, in English, Spanish, Dari/Farsi, and Swahili. A chatbot, not a person and not legal advice, but a solid first stop at 2am when the office is closed: welcome.rescue.org/us/en/services/alma
8

Free food, the local circuit

Weekly · pinned map, directions, and live searches✓▶

Explore a more cinematic raised-terrain Florida itinerary inspired by premium travel dioramas. The dark route plaque and Broward close-up both have tappable stops, while every location retains direct Google Maps directions and live searches before travel.

Florida · Broward County · Oakland Park · Hollywood
Premium Florida food itinerary
A richer miniature Florida sculpture with landmarks, luminous routing, a travel-style itinerary plaque, and a magnified Broward route. Tap any numbered stop.
3 stops · premium 3D
◉ Interactive terrain
Illustrative premium terrain model for orientation. The Florida sculpture and landmarks are visual; use the Google Maps buttons for live navigation, traffic, distance, and current routing.
🧭 Plan the 3-stop route ⌕ Search pantries near me

Search another ZIP code or city

Edit the search phrase below; Google Maps opens outside the app with nearby pantry results.

1

Cathedral of Prayer Ministries · Fellowship Hall

Mon to Thu · hours vary

200 NE 35 Street, Oakland Park, FL 33334 · hall by the church at 251 NE 34 Court.

Community reports mention bread, bakery items, and occasional meat trays. Check the latest post before driving.

📍 Google directions ⌕ Check latest
2

Blessed Sacrament Church

Friday · 1:30 to 3:15 PM

1701 E Oakland Park Blvd, Oakland Park, FL 33334.

Food distribution is community-listed for Friday afternoon. Call or search before travel because pantry schedules can change.

📍 Google directions ⌕ Check latest
3

Grace's Closet · Serving With GRACE

Tuesday · 4:00 PM until gone

2035 Harding St, Suite 101, Hollywood, FL 33020.

First come, first served; arrive early and check the newest post because dates and available items change.

📍 Google directions ⌕ Check latest
Feeding South Florida pantry directorySearch current programs by location and filters.↗ Live distribution calendarReview the day-of schedule before attending.↗ Search local community giveawaysFood and household pickups are often shared locally.↗ Dial 2-1-1Ask for food pantries and pop-up distributions near your ZIP.☎
Confirm before you travel. Pantry days, times, eligibility, and stock can change quickly, especially “until gone” distributions. Use the Google “Check latest” button or the live Feeding South Florida locator on the same day.
Make it a route: combine one pantry stop, community pickups, and a grocery top-up. Pantry food does not reduce the value of stretching Matching Grant cash for rent, transport, utilities, and other essentials.
9

Driving and transport

Learner test, transport support, license, then the car✓▶
RIDE
Transport support is here

Bus passes, Uber, learner test, and a road-test car

Open the support plan before paying for rides or a vehicle. It explains agency transport requests, Broward programs, the Uber tracker, license steps, and road-test rental checks.

Open transport support Open learner test Open Uber tracker
1

Driving learner test + driver's license

Week 3 onward▶

The SA license does not convert, you run the Florida process.

  • Use the supplied Florida Premium DMV Test Cheat Sheet as a third-party practice aid, then confirm every rule against the official handbook.
  • Use the official FLHSMV handbook and Class E practice test, and repeat practice questions until you pass consistently.
  • Sequence: if required, complete TLSAE, take the knowledge exam, complete vision and hearing screening, then take the driving skills test.
TEST
Driving learner test

Study, practise, then book the Florida Class E test

The full built-in learner test guide and the supplied 59 page cheat sheet are available here under Driving. The PDF is embedded inside this HTML, so it works even when the separate PDF file is not beside the app.

Start learner test Open cheat sheet in app Save PDF Open transport support
PDF

Florida learner cheat sheet

The supplied 59-page study guide is included with the built-in trainer. It covers document preparation, exam basics, and 150 practice questions with explanations.

Open 59 page PDF in app Start learner test Save cheat sheet Study pack details
Embedded in this app. No separate PDF link or folder setup is required.
📎 Same source pack as the Florida ID (passport, EAD, SSN, I-94, two address proofs), plus the Florida ID itself once issued.
2

Car

After license + first income▶

South Florida is hard without a car, but do not rush into financing with no credit history, the rates offered to new arrivals are brutal.

  • Bridge with bus passes (assistance programs cover these), lifts from volunteers, and rideshare.
  • Buy used, cash if possible, after the license and first paychecks. Check every VIN's history before paying: Carfax (carfax.com, paid report, accidents, owners, odometer, service records, many dealers hand it over free if you ask). Free complements: NICB VINCheck (theft + salvage) and NHTSA recalls (nhtsa.gov/recalls). A seller who refuses the VIN or the report is the answer, walk away.
  • Insurance is mandatory in Florida and quotes vary wildly, get at least three.
Assistance programs will not help buy a car. Transportation support means bus passes or petrol money only.
Transport bridge before the car: ask your caseworker, in writing, what transport support is available for bus passes, petrol vouchers, volunteer trip sheets, or a county low-income transport program. Use Uber or Lyft only when the bus will not work for interviews, medical appointments, food pickups, or late shifts, then track every ride in the Daily life rides tool. For the license road test, compare a driving-school road-test car such as BBM Driving School and Transportation LLC with public rental companies such as Hertz, Enterprise, Avis, Budget, Alamo, National, Dollar, and Thrifty, but verify learner-permit and test-use rules before paying.
3

Transport support

Program help, Uber, license, and road-test car▶
Transport bridge plan

Do this before the car purchase

Use the program support first, Uber second, and the Florida license path as the permanent solution. Keep requests in writing and keep screenshots of vouchers, bus passes, and ride receipts.

1
Ask for transport supportAsk your caseworker or Job Developer whether your case has bus passes, petrol vouchers, volunteer trip sheets, or public-transport vouchers. In Broward, also ask about the Transportation Disadvantaged bus-pass program.
2
Use Uber as the gap fillerUse Uber or Lyft for interviews, medical visits, food pickups, late shifts, or routes the bus cannot cover. Track rides in the Daily life cost tool so the real weekly spend does not disappear.
3
Make the license the milestonePrioritise the Florida Class E knowledge test, road test, insurance quotes, and then a modest used car. A car before the license and first paychecks usually traps the budget.
Open driving learner test Open Uber tracker Road-test car rental Broward TD bus pass TOPS paratransit info
Broward example: the Transportation Disadvantaged bus-pass application uses 225% of the Federal Poverty Guidelines and asks for government ID plus financial documents. TOPS paratransit and Rider's Choice are separate eligibility tracks. Call BCT at 954-357-8400 and verify before relying on any route or benefit.
Road-test vehicle plan

Rent only after you verify the rules

For the Florida road test you need a registered, insured, road-worthy vehicle with working lights, signals, horn, mirrors, brakes, seatbelts, and valid documents. Do not assume a normal rental counter will work: many public rental companies rent only to licensed drivers, not learner-permit holders, and some contracts restrict use for a driving test. Call first and ask exactly.

A
Use a driving-school road-test car when neededA driving school or road-test rental service is often the cleaner option because they can confirm the vehicle is insured, registered, road-worthy, and acceptable for the skills test.
B
Call public rental companies as backupsCompare known brands such as Hertz, Enterprise, Avis, Budget, Alamo, National, Dollar, and Thrifty, but ask about learner permits, road-test use, insurance, deposit, age rules, and who is allowed to drive.
C
Bring the document checklistFor any road-test vehicle, bring registration, insurance proof, rental agreement if rented, your learner permit or ID, appointment confirmation, and the full FLHSMV identity/address pack.
BBM Driving School and Transportation LLCDriving lessons, driving tests, and car rental for the license process. Phone: 754-204-2991. User-supplied business contact. Verify price, pickup location, insurance, registration, instructor availability, and road-test eligibility before booking.
HertzPublic rental company to compare for licensed-driver needs. Confirm road-test use and learner-permit rules before paying.
hertz.com
EnterprisePublic rental company to compare. Ask about age, license status, deposit, insurance, and whether the vehicle can be used for a driving test.
enterprise.com
Avis / BudgetPublic rental brands to compare. Confirm the contract allows the exact driver and purpose before booking.
avis.com / budget.com
Alamo / NationalPublic rental brands to compare for airport or local pickup. Do not rely on them for the road test until they confirm learner-permit and test-use rules.
alamo.com / nationalcar.com
Dollar / ThriftyBudget rental brands to compare. Check insurance, deposit, extra-driver rules, and road-test restrictions before handing over money.
dollar.com / thrifty.com
Road-test warning: a cheap rental is not useful if the examiner refuses the car. Confirm with the rental provider and the driver license office before the appointment. Keep screenshots and receipts.
  • Driver licence timeline: most states make you get a local licence soon after you live or work there (Florida: within 30 days of establishing residency, taking a job, or enrolling kids in school). South Africa is not on the US reciprocity list, so you sit the written knowledge test and the road test, plus a vision check. Your licence is issued valid to your immigration status date. Book the appointment early.
  • Buying your first car: budget the total, not the sticker: price + sales tax (~6 to 7% FL) + title + tag + insurance. Pick a modest, common, reliable used model (cheap parts and repairs). Always get an independent pre-purchase inspection and run the VIN for title and accident history. Private sale is cheaper than a dealer but has no warranty. Avoid "buy here, pay here" lots.
  • Insurance shopping: Florida is no-fault; the legal minimum is PIP $10,000 + Property Damage $10,000, but the minimum is not enough real cover. With no US driving record your first premiums are high, and a foreign licence plus thin credit pushes them higher. Get 3 to 4 quotes, ask whether they credit a clean international driving record, raise the deductible to lower the premium, and re-shop every 6 to 12 months, it drops a lot as your US record builds. Yes, the first quote reads like creative fiction. It comes down.
  • Registration: title and register at the Florida DHSMV / tax collector. New residents pay a one-time ~$225 fee plus title and plate fees, then renew annually. You need Florida insurance in place first.
  • Before the car: Broward County Transit (BCT) buses, Tri-Rail (Miami to West Palm), and Brightline cover a lot. A monthly bus pass beats car + insurance in year one, and many employers sit on a bus route. Add the BCT phone number to your contacts and ask your agency whether transport support is recurring monthly or front-loaded.
Driving referenceFlorida learner test and license study packThe full test trainer and supplied cheat sheet now live inside Driving.
DL

Driving learner test (Florida Class E)

Inside activity 9, Driving and transport▶

Everything to pass the Florida Class E knowledge exam and the road test, built from the official handbook. The questions below are study prompts in our own words with the answers, grouped by topic. For the exact official wording and the answer key, use the free Official Florida Driver License Handbook and the answer key at flhsmv.gov/handbooks-manuals.

Supplied learner resource | 59 pages | May 2026

Florida Premium DMV Test Cheat Sheet

Use this third-party PDF as a practice companion to the built-in Florida Class E trainer and the official handbook. It includes the application sequence, document reminders, exam structure, and 150 question-and-answer explanations.

Document checklistPrimary identity, Social Security evidence, two Florida address proofs, and immigration documents when applicable.
Knowledge exam pattern50 multiple-choice questions, with 40 correct answers needed to pass.
Practice bank150 explained questions covering safe driving, laws, signs, road markings, emergencies, and vehicle handling.
Verify before testingFees, languages, appointments, and rules can change. Current FLHSMV guidance takes priority.
Preview PDF in app Open PDF full screen Share or save PDF Open official Class E page Open 2026 exam-language notice
Important: The supplied PDF is a third-party study aid and remains the publisher's copyrighted material. Some language, fee, or rule notes may lag current Florida guidance. Use the official FLHSMV page and handbook as the final authority.
The full 59 page PDF is embedded inside every HTML copy. The separate PDF is also included in the release folder as a backup.
How the exam works:
  • 50 multiple-choice questions on traffic law, road signs/signals/markings and safe driving. Pass = 40 correct (80%).
  • Adults 18+ test in person at a driver license or tax-collector office (teens may test online).
  • Exam language: FLHSMV announced that beginning 6 Feb 2026 all knowledge and skills exams are administered in English only, without translation services or interpreters. Some older FLHSMV pages may still list other languages, so confirm the current policy when booking.
  • TLSAE first: if you have never held a driver license in any country, you must complete the TLSAE (Traffic Law & Substance Abuse Education) course before applying. If you held a foreign licence (e.g. a SA one), bring proof, it may exempt you from TLSAE. South Africa is not a reciprocity country, so you still take the knowledge test and the road test.
  • Then the driving skills (road) test in a registered, insured, road-worthy vehicle. Bring what is listed at flhsmv.gov/WhatToBring.
Do it online: MyDMV Portal + the app. MyDMVPortal.flhsmv.gov and the MyFlorida (FLHSMV) app (App Store / Google Play) let you renew or replace a licence/ID, renew vehicle registration, change address, order your driving record, check licence status, download the handbook, find exam providers, order a paper title, get a crash report, view live road conditions, and reinstate a licence.
FLHSMV contacts: Customer Service (850) 617-2000 · website flhsmv.gov · online transactions MyDMVPortal.flhsmv.gov · what to bring flhsmv.gov/WhatToBring · handbook + answer key flhsmv.gov/handbooks-manuals · crash reports flhsmv.gov/traffic-crash-reports.
1Before you drive: the car & you▶
  • Adjust before you start the engine: seat, all mirrors, head restraint, then seatbelt. Know where your blind spots are.
  • Equipment that must work: head/tail/brake lights, turn signals, horn, wipers, at least one mirror, a muffler; windshield and windows clear (no stickers blocking your view, no TV the driver can see).
  • Seatbelts: the driver, all front-seat passengers and everyone under 18 must be belted, in any seat. If a front-seat passenger under 18 is unbelted, the driver is charged. It is a primary offence (they can stop you just for that).
  • Child restraints: ages 0 to 3 in a separate carrier or the car's integrated seat; ages 4 to 5 a carrier, integrated seat or seatbelt; all children 5 and under must be restrained.
  • A load sticking out more than 4 feet behind the vehicle must be marked with a red flag by day and a red light at night.
  • Drowsy / impaired: never drive tired; after drinking, judgment and vision go first. There is no safe amount before driving, and no one can drink and drive safely.
  • Distracted driving has 3 categories: visual (eyes off road), manual (hands off wheel), cognitive (mind off driving). Texting is all three and is the most dangerous; it is banned while driving in Florida.
2Signs, signals & road markings▶
  • Sign colours: red = stop / prohibition, yellow = general warning, orange = construction/maintenance, green = guidance/directions, blue = driver services, brown = recreation, white = regulatory (e.g. speed).
  • Yellow lines separate opposing traffic: solid yellow on your side = no passing; double solid yellow = no passing either way; broken yellow = pass when clear.
  • White lines separate same-direction traffic: single broken white = lane divider, change when safe; double solid white = no lane change.
  • Traffic lights: steady red = full stop; right on red after a full stop unless a sign forbids it; flashing red = treat as a stop sign; flashing yellow = slow and proceed with caution; green arrow = protected turn.
  • Flashing yellow at a railroad crossing without gates: slow down, look both ways, be ready to stop, and never cross if a train is coming.
3Right of way & intersections▶
  • Open (uncontrolled) intersection: yield to any vehicle already in it and to the vehicle on your right; turning left, yield to oncoming traffic.
  • 4-way stop: first to arrive goes first; if you arrive together, the one on the right goes; when unsure, yield.
  • Pedestrians: always yield in crosswalks. A pedestrian with a white cane or guide dog is visually impaired, stop and let them cross.
  • Emergency vehicle approaching with lights/siren: pull to the nearest right edge, clear of intersections, and stop until it passes.
  • Move Over Law: for a stopped emergency or service vehicle with flashing lights, move over a lane; if you cannot, slow to 20 mph below the posted limit.
  • School bus, red lights + stop arm: stop if you are behind it, or oncoming on an undivided road. On a divided highway with a raised barrier, oncoming traffic need not stop; traffic behind always stops.
4Speed, following & lane use▶
  • Speed: obey posted limits; where unposted, defaults apply (e.g. ~30 mph residential, less in school/work zones). Driving too slowly can also get you cited for impeding traffic.
  • Double your speed and the force of impact and your stopping distance roughly quadruple.
  • Following distance: use the 4-second rule; add more in rain, fog, heavy traffic, at night, when tailgated, or behind a motorcycle.
  • Blind spots: look over your shoulder before changing lanes or turning, mirrors do not show everything.
  • Passing: pass on the left when the line and traffic allow; pull back in once you can see the passed vehicle in your mirror; do not speed to pass.
  • Turns & signals: signal at least 100 feet ahead, get into the correct lane early, and never turn from the wrong lane.
  • Limited-access highway: use the acceleration lane to reach traffic speed before merging; leave only at marked exits.
5Sharing the road▶
  • Motorcycles & mopeds have the same rights and duties as cars. Give them a full lane, never share one.
  • Cyclists: give at least 3 feet when passing; when turning, yield to a cyclist in the bike lane and turn behind them.
  • "No Zone" = a large truck's blind spots (front, rear and both sides). Do not linger there; trucks need far more room to stop and make wide right turns, never squeeze up their right side.
6Lights & visibility▶
  • Headlights on from sunset to sunrise and in any rain, smoke or fog. Rule of thumb: wipers on, lights on. Parking lights do not count.
  • Dim your high beams within 500 feet of an oncoming vehicle and within 300 feet when following another vehicle.
  • Rain, fog or smoke in daytime: use low beams, not high, high beams reflect back and cut your vision.
  • Oncoming high beams: do not stare at them, watch the right edge of your lane until they pass.
7Bad weather & emergencies▶
  • Wet roads / hydroplaning: slow down; if the car starts to float, ease off the gas, do not brake hard.
  • Skid: ease off the accelerator and steer gently where you want the front to go; do not slam the brakes.
  • Right wheels off the pavement: do not jerk the wheel, ease off the gas, hold straight, slow down, then steer back gently.
  • Braking: with ABS, hold firm steady pressure and steer (it pumps for you); without ABS, pump the brakes.
  • Tyre blowout: grip the wheel, ease off the gas, do not brake hard, coast off the road to stop.
  • Minor crash blocking traffic: if the car is drivable, move it off the roadway, then exchange information and report as required.
  • Leaving the scene of a crash with injury or death is a serious felony (hit-and-run), heavy fines, licence loss and possible prison.
  • Hit a parked car and cannot find the owner: leave a note with your name and contact details and report it.
8Insurance & the law (Florida)▶
  • No-Fault Law: before you register a vehicle you must carry at least $10,000 PIP (Personal Injury Protection) + $10,000 PDL (Property Damage Liability).
  • Suspended under the No-Fault / Financial Responsibility Law: reinstate by buying the required insurance and paying the reinstatement fee. At fault and uninsured, you can be personally liable for the damage.
  • DUI: illegal at 0.08 BAC (0.02 under 21, 0.04 commercial); you can still be charged below 0.08 if impaired.
  • Implied consent: refusing a breath, blood or urine test = automatic 1-year suspension (first refusal), 18 months + a misdemeanour for a later refusal.
  • Racing on the highway: licence revocation, fines, vehicle impound and possible jail.
9Licensing, teens & courses▶
  • Class E knowledge exam: 50 questions, pass at 80% (40/50).
  • TLSAE is required before a first licence if you have never been licensed anywhere; under-18 take the DETS course instead.
  • Learner's licence: log 50 hours of supervised driving (10 at night) before the Class E driving test; first 3 months daylight only, then until 10 pm, with a licensed adult 21+ in the front seat.
  • Teen curfews: 16-year-olds may not drive 11 pm to 6 am; 17-year-olds may not drive 1 am to 5 am (work and parent exceptions).
  • Health: you must notify FLHSMV of conditions that can affect your driving (vision, seizures, etc.).
  • Basic Driver Improvement (BDI): take it voluntarily after a non-criminal moving violation to keep points off your record and avoid an insurance increase (once per 12 months).
10The driving (skills) test▶
  • Bring a registered, insured, road-worthy vehicle.
  • Manoeuvres tested: smooth starting and stopping, backing straight, turns, lane changes with proper observation and signalling, parking (including parallel and on a hill), obeying signs and signals, and safe speed.
  • Parking on a hill: downhill, turn the wheels toward the curb; uphill with a curb, turn away from the curb; uphill or downhill with no curb, turn the wheels toward the road edge.
  • Three-point turn: to reverse direction on a narrow two-way road when there is no other option, signal, check traffic, complete it in three controlled moves.
10

Schooling

Address-dependent · apply early✓▶

Public school is assigned by your housing address, and honestly, the public options here are not always strong. Look wider:

  • Step Up For Students (stepupforstudents.org): Florida scholarship programs that fund private school and home schooling, refugee families generally qualify on income. Apply as early as possible, funds allocate through the year.
  • School registration needs: proof of address, vaccination records (the medicals in step 5 feed this), birth certificate/passport.
  • Ask the school about ESOL support if needed, and free/reduced meals.
💉Standard school vaccines (Form DH 680)▶

Florida law requires proof of certain vaccines before a child can start school (Pre-K to grade 12). The proof is the DH 680, Florida Certification of Immunization, completed by a Florida-licensed provider and printed from Florida SHOTS, the state registry. No valid DH 680, no attendance.

VaccineDoses
DTaP / DTP (diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis)4 to 5
Polio (IPV)4 to 5
Hepatitis B3
MMR (measles, mumps, rubella)2
Varicella (chickenpox)2
Tdap booster (grades 7 to 12)1
Catch-up is common for new arrivals. Some non-US doses do not count toward Florida’s requirements. The oral polio vaccine (OPV) given in many countries often does NOT count, so your child may need US polio (IPV) doses to comply. Expect a few catch-up shots.

How to get the DH 680: bring all overseas shot records (translated into English) to a Florida provider or county health department. They review the history, give any missing doses, and complete the form. Overseas records on their own are not accepted, a Florida provider must certify the DH 680.

  • Mid-series? The provider can issue a Temporary Medical Exemption on the DH 680, with an expiry date, so your child can start while finishing the schedule.
  • You also need the School Entry Health Exam (DH 3040), a physical, separate from the shots form.
  • Not required for Florida school but recommended by the CDC: HPV, meningococcal, COVID, flu. Optional, ask your doctor.
Book the well-child visit in spring or early summer so the DH 680 is ready before the school year starts. Schools send children home without a valid form.
📎 Bring: child's passport · I-94 · immunization records · lease (school zoning runs off the address).
11

YOUR REFUGEE BUDGET, the PIR money

Know these numbers before week 2✓▶
PIR budget
$2,450
per person, once-off, any age
Agency flex holdback
$450
per person, may go to other refugees
Period
90 days
or until funds depleted, first of the two
Family of 3 example
$7,350
$6,000 if all flex is held back

How it works. Your Program of Initial Resettlement budget is $2,450 per refugee, whether the person is 1 or 99. It is once-off, not monthly. Some agencies hold back the $450 flex portion per person in an agency account, they do not have to spend it on you.

Where it goes. Your agency spends it on your behalf: rent deposit, rent, utility deposits, utilities, hotel stay, the starter furniture and household items, food, and a small amount of cash assistance. Most payments go directly to the landlord and utility companies, expect to see very little cash.

If you find a job fast and cover your own costs: at the end of the 90 days, ask (by email) for the full expenditure report and receipts, and request a check for any remaining balance. It is your allocation, audit it.

After PIR. When the 90 days end or the money runs out, whichever comes first, there is always a follow-on program if you still need it: Matching Grant, PC Grant, TANF, refugee cash assistance. All of them carry employment requirements, and all of them cover only basics: rent, utilities, food allowance, transportation (bus passes or petrol money, never a car).

Typical follow-on amounts (Matching Grant example)

ItemAmount
Food, per adult per month$200
Food, per child per month$40
Petrol/gas per month$80
Amounts step down as you earn. Checks are for specific things, not general cash.
Nothing is offered automatically. Recertification courses, child care, work clothes, school uniforms, food banks, diapers, extra funding, it all exists, but only surfaces when you ask, specifically, by email. Ask ASAP.
About the pressure. Agencies only have to offer you an entry-level job, and they have employment metrics to hit. Expect lines like "your money is now finished", "take any job now", "pay rent at month-end or leave". Do not panic, it is motivation pressure, not an eviction notice. Run your own race: resume ready, network, job fairs, interviews, and find the best-paying job you can, as fast as you can, instead of being pushed into the first counter job.
📈Build US credit from zero▶

You arrive with no US credit history. Your South African credit does not transfer, and the usual import tools do not cover South Africa. A US credit score (FICO, 300 to 850) decides apartment approvals, car loans, deposits, even some jobs. You build it from zero, and with discipline you can reach a usable score in 12 to 24 months.

What makes up your FICO score

Payment history35%
Amounts owed (utilization)30%
Length of credit history15%
Types of credit10%
New credit / inquiries10%

The first two, payment history and how much of your limit you use, are 65% of your score. Win those two and the rest follows.

Build it from zero, in order

  • 1. Get your SSN first. Nothing reports to the bureaus without it.
  • 2. Open a US bank account (checking + savings). New here? Book an in-person appointment, it is the foundation for direct deposit, a secured card, everything.
  • 3. Get a secured credit card. You put down a deposit (say $200 to $500) that becomes your limit. It reports to the bureaus and builds history. A prepaid card does NOT, it never reports.
  • 4. Become an authorized user on an established card if a trusted person will add you, their good history can lift yours.
  • 5. Keep utilization low. Use under 30% of your limit, under 10% is better. This alone is 30% of your score.
  • 6. Pay every bill on time, in full, every month. Payment history is the single biggest factor. Automate at least the minimum so you never miss.
  • 7. Do not open several accounts at once. Each application is a hard inquiry, many in a short window reads as risk.
  • 8. Do not close your oldest card. Length of history and total available credit both matter, closing shrinks both.
  • 9. Add Experian Boost. It counts on-time utility, phone and streaming payments you already make.
  • 10. Check and monitor for free. One free report a year at AnnualCreditReport.com, and watch your FICO free in your bank app.
Use the bank’s free credit education (for example Bank of America’s Better Money Habits). Never pay a “credit repair” service to do what you can do yourself for free.
🧾SA → US tax: get a dual-qualified adviser▶

For at least your first year you have a foot in two tax systems at once, and the gap between them is where the cost lands. Sort this early, the penalties for getting it wrong are steep.

Why it matters:

  • SARS may still treat you as SA tax-resident until you formally cease residency, so SA can still tax you while the US also starts taxing your worldwide income once you are a US tax resident.
  • Your SA and offshore bank and retirement accounts trigger US foreign-account reporting (FBAR and FATCA), separate from your tax return, with heavy penalties for missing them.
  • Ceasing SA residency and moving money can trigger SA exit tax (a deemed disposal) and SARB / forex steps. Retirement funds have their own rules and lock-ups.

What to do: engage one adviser qualified in BOTH SA and US tax (a cross-border, dual-qualified practitioner), ideally before your first US filing season. The US tax year runs January to December and returns are due around April. A SA-only accountant or a US-only CPA, used alone, will each miss the other half.

Ask any adviser to cover: SARS cessation of tax residency and exit tax · the SA to US double-tax agreement · FBAR (FinCEN 114) and FATCA (Form 8938) on your SA and offshore accounts · how your SA retirement funds are treated · forex and SARB rules on moving funds.

📎 Cross-border tax help: use a licensed adviser who can document experience with both South African and US tax rules. Verify credentials, fees, privacy practices, and the written scope before sharing financial documents. No private individual contact details are embedded in this app.
🧺Track laundry & ride costs▶

No washing machine yet, and no car at first? Laundromat trips and Uber rides add up fast in the first months. Track them here to see your real weekly spend. Everything is saved only on your phone, nothing is shared.

Transport program check: before paying for repeated Uber rides, ask for the transport line in writing. In Broward, ask about the Transportation Disadvantaged bus pass and TOPS paratransit separately. Use the Rides tab below for every Uber or Lyft trip so the budget shows the truth.
Avg / trip
$0.00
0 trips
This trip
$0.00

Tap each price and set it to your laundromat’s rate per load, prices vary a lot. Then tap + for how many loads. Use + Machine to add a big washer or extra dryer.

Uber, round trip
$ there and back

Trips

Total rides
$0.00
0 rides
This month
$0.00

Rides

12

The flight payback agreement

First bill ~month 5 · payments from month 6✓▶

The flights to America were not free, they were a loan. Before departure, every adult signed an IOM promissory note covering the airfare for everyone on the case. It is real debt, but on the friendliest terms you will ever see in this country:

  • 0% interest, forever. The amount on the note is the amount you repay, nothing more.
  • Nothing is due for the first ~6 months. The first billing statement typically arrives around month 5, payments generally start at month 6 after arrival.
  • 36 months to repay, in monthly installments set out on the note (roughly the total divided by 36). Statements come by mail from the resettlement agency named on your note, payments go to them.
  • Pay on time and it BUILDS your credit: travel loan payments are reported to the credit bureaus, for most refugee families this is literally the first line of US credit history, months before any secured card. Treat it as an asset, not a burden.
  • Miss payments and it bites: 4+ months unpaid, or not settled within 46 months, and the loan can be accelerated and sent to a collection agency, which wrecks the same credit file it could have built.
  • Cannot pay a month? Email the loan office BEFORE the due date, not after. Deferment and renegotiated schedules exist, but only for people who ask in writing while still in good standing.
  • Moving? Send the loan office your new address the same week. Statements chasing an old address are the #1 way families slide into accidental default.
Set up your repayment account, do not wait for the first statement: you request the repayment agreement and manage the loan yourself.

1. IOM Travel Loan info: iom.int/travel-loans
2. Create your account + pay online: IOM Consumer Payment Portal, consumer.iom.int/auth/login (register first if you have no account). The portal shows your outstanding balance and payment history, lets you view and print your signed promissory note, and lets you update your contact details, which is exactly where the new-address rule above gets done.
3. Questions: email iomloans@iom.int or call 1-866-466-5660 (toll-free).
Three ways to pay each installment (the statement spells them out, repayment runs over 36 months):
  1. Online portal, by card. Pay on the IOM Consumer Portal above; you enter your card number and CVV. Fastest, but if you would rather not type card details into the portal, options 2 and 3 avoid that.
  2. Cheque by post, mailed in the self-addressed envelope enclosed with your paper statement.
  3. Money order by post, mailed the same way.
New to US post and money orders?
  • Stamps: Walgreens, CVS, Walmart, Target, most grocery stores, the USPS lobby self-service kiosk, or online. Drop the sealed envelope in any USPS box or your complex outgoing-mail slot.
  • Money orders: buy one at most retail stores, banks, or post offices. Usually capped at $1,000 each, paid upfront with cash or a debit card, fee about $1 to $5. If an installment is over $1,000 you split it across two.
Watch the file number. Your IOM loan file number is often different from your resettlement case number. Pay against, and quote, the loan file number printed on the statement, not the case number, so the money lands on the right account.
Budget move: put the monthly installment into your budget (previous milestone) from month 4, two months before the first payment is due. When the first statement lands, it is already a line item, not a surprise.
📎 Source document: your signed promissory note copy (each adult signed one). It states the exact total, the monthly amount, and the agency that collects. Lives at home with the originals.
13

Know your rights, grievance + escalation

Read before you need it✓▶

The golden rule: always friendly, always respectful, never a doormat. Know your rights, and push back when it matters, especially on the apartment lease.

After every discussion, send the email. Phone call, hallway chat, home visit, it does not exist until you write it down. Same day, short recap: "Per our conversation today, we agreed X by Y date." If they do not correct it, it stands as the record. This one habit wins more disputes than any argument.

Your rights in the program include:

  • A copy of the agency's written grievance procedure, ask for it at intake, they are required to have one and tell you about it.
  • The full expenditure report and receipts for your PIR budget, it is spent on your behalf, you may audit it.
  • Housing that is safe and reasonable for your family size and condition issues fixed, do not accept a 1-bedroom for a family with a child without pushing back, and never accept verbal-only promises on the lease, pet permission, deposits, or repairs.
  • Saying no to signing anything you have not read or do not understand, ask for time, ask for it in writing.
  • An interpreter if you need one, free.

The escalation ladder, climb it one rung at a time, in writing:

  • 1. Caseworker. Raise it by email, specific, dated, with what you are asking for and by when. Give a reasonable window to respond, a few working days.
  • 2. Supervisor. No answer or no fix, reply on the same email thread and copy the caseworker's supervisor, ask for the supervisor's name at intake so you have it before you need it.
  • 3. Agency grievance officer. Every resettlement agency has a designated grievance contact and a formal process. Invoke it by name: "I would like to file a formal grievance under your grievance procedure."
  • 4. Agency head office. Each national agency (CWS, IRC, HIAS, etc.) has a headquarters that oversees local affiliates, their contact is on the agency website.
  • 5. State Refugee Coordinator / ORR. The federal Office of Refugee Resettlement funds the program, every state has a refugee coordinator's office that takes complaints about affiliates. This is the top rung, you rarely need it, but knowing it exists changes conversations.
Keep it on email, keep it polite, keep the thread. One thread per issue, never start fresh threads, the history is your evidence. Stay factual: dates, amounts, what was promised, what happened. Anger in writing only weakens your file. Friendly and relentless beats furious and forgettable.
Disputes are the exception, not the rule. Most families never go past rung 1. The volunteers and your agency genuinely want you to land well, escalation is the seatbelt, not the steering wheel.
14

Grievance policy, get it in writing

Ask for it the day you need leverage✓▶
Real example from a family in the program: they were told the caseworker is "not allowed" to share staff email addresses. That answer is itself the signal. The counter is simple: email the office immediately and ask for their grievance policy. A formal grievance request cannot be handled verbally, it forces the written channel open, and the policy document names exactly who you may contact and how.

Copy-paste email, send it the same day:

Subject: Request, written grievance policy

Dear [office],

Please send me a copy of your written grievance policy and the contact details for the person designated to receive grievances, as provided to clients under your program. Please confirm receipt of this email.

Kind regards, [name], case [number]

If your agency is the IRC (International Rescue Committee), the documents are public:

  • IRC HOME Program Manual (the rulebook your services run under):
    rescue.org · IRC HOME Program Manual 1.0 (PDF)
  • IRC Code of Conduct, the IRC Way (integrity, service, accountability, equality, binding on all staff, volunteers and partners, available in 18 languages):
    rescue.org/page/our-code-conduct
  • IRC Ethics Hotline (report a violation, confidential, anonymous option, phone or online):
    rescue.org/page/ethics-hotline-report-issue
  • IRC EthicsPoint portal (the official online reporting system the hotline runs on, yes it is a third-party domain, it is legitimate):
    secure.ethicspoint.com · IRC reporting portal
Reporting a problem does NOT get you removed from the program

The rules protect you, not the other way around:

1. Your benefits are federal. ORR-funded programs belong to the federal government, not to one caseworker or even the local agency. A caseworker cannot cancel your refugee status or take your benefits because you complained.

2. You have the right to a supervisor. Every resettlement agency must have a supervisor, a written grievance process, and a route to higher management. Refusing to let you speak to a supervisor is not normal practice, it is itself a red flag.

3. Reporting leads to review, not punishment. The normal outcome is: case reviewed, problem corrected, sometimes a different caseworker assigned. Refugees are not punished for asking for help.

4. You cannot be made homeless without process. ORR-funded agencies are expected to help with housing placement and access to benefits and basic services. "Your money is finished, go live on the street" or "pay the rent yourself by month-end or leave" is not how the program works, support is a bridge to self-sufficiency with defined stages, not a trapdoor.

Know the actual support stages, so nobody can bluff you about them:

  • Stage 1, initial resettlement (first ~30 to 90 days): the agency receives a one-time per-capita grant (in the region of $2,450 per refugee) to secure an apartment, basic furnishings, food, some cash assistance, and the first rent and utilities.
  • Stage 2, months ~4 to 8: if not yet self-sufficient, time-limited cash assistance continues through Refugee Cash Assistance (for those outside TANF) or the Matching Grant program (120 to 240 days, usually with more intensive housing support, the MG milestone covers it in full).
  • Stage 3, up to 5 years: direct rent payments end within the first year, but Refugee Support Services remain: housing referrals, advocacy with landlords, and help if you face eviction or a housing crisis. High-need cases can get intensive case management for up to a year through the Preferred Communities program.
Email only, always. Do not rely on anything said or texted. If it is true, they can write it in an email. If they will not put it in an email, treat it as not said.

The escalation ladder when something is genuinely wrong:

  • First: ask your agency, by email, for its grievance procedure (template above) and follow it. Most issues die here.
  • Then, for serious unresolved issues (unsafe housing, no food or money on arrival, pressure or intimidation to sign a lease immediately, racist or political abuse, entitled funding or services withheld, your caseworker and agency ignoring you): email the federal grievance team directly at RefugeeProgramsGrievance@acf.hhs.gov, the ORR Refugee Programs Grievance Response Team. Send the required personal details directly to the official recipient, not through this app. Include your case number, agency name and address, and describe the issues concisely, facts and dates, not emotion.

If your agency is CWS, HIAS, or another affiliate: every resettlement agency operating under the program is required to maintain a grievance procedure and inform clients of it. The same email above works word for word, just send it to your affiliate's office address. If the local office stalls, the national headquarters contact is on the agency's website, and the ethics/whistleblower channel exists at every one of them.

Sequence it with step 17: the grievance policy request is rung 3 of the ladder made concrete. You do not need to be angry to ask for it, asking for the policy politely, in writing, with a read-receipt request, changes the temperature of every conversation that follows. Most issues resolve within days of that one email.
Why staff emails matter: verbal-only communication keeps everything deniable. The program runs on documentation, yours should too. If individual emails are genuinely withheld, the office email plus your one-thread-per-issue habit (step 17) achieves the same record.
15

Daily life: convert & translate

Reference · type any number, no ticking✓▶

US daily life runs on miles, pounds, gallons, inches and Fahrenheit, and on a few words that mean something different here. Tap a topic, type any value, get the answer both ways.

Convert it · live calculators
Type in either box. The paired value updates immediately. Values are not stored.
1Temperature▶
⇄
0 °C32 °F
20 °C68 °F
30 °C86 °F
37 °C98.6 °F

Anchors: freeze 0°C=32°F, boil 100°C=212°F. A pleasant 75°F ≈ 24°C.

2Distance▶
⇄
1 km0.62 mi
10 km6.21 mi
100 km62.14 mi

Roads: speed limits in mph. 25 in town, 65 to 70 on the freeway.

3Length▶
⇄
1 cm0.39 in
30 cm11.81 in
100 cm39.37 in

Height in feet+inches: 1.80 m ≈ 5 ft 11 in. 12 in = 1 ft.

4Weight▶
⇄
1 kg2.20 lb
10 kg22.05 lb
50 kg110.23 lb

Deli/meat sold per pound (lb). 1 lb ≈ 450 g.

5Volume▶
⇄
1 L0.26 gal
10 L2.64 gal
50 L13.21 gal

US gallon ≈ 3.79 L. Petrol priced per gallon, so a "$3.50" pump is per gallon.

6Fuel economy▶
⇄
5 L/10047.0 mpg
8 L/10029.4 mpg
12 L/10019.6 mpg

Flipped: in SA lower L/100km is better, in the US higher MPG is better. 30+ mpg is economical.

7Tyre pressure▶
⇄
200 kPa29.0 psi
250 kPa36.3 psi
300 kPa43.5 psi

Tyres in PSI: typical car 32 to 35 psi. Correct number is on a sticker inside the driver’s door.

8Clothing sizes▶
2
US size

Men’s tops stay the same (M=M, L=L). Men’s trousers use waist inches, e.g. 34.

9Shoe sizes▶
7
US men
5
US women

Roughly +1 size SA/UK to US. Brands vary, try on where you can.

10Money▶
⇄

Rates move daily. Enter the live rate from xe.com or your bank first. Rough gut check: divide rands by about 18.

11Date format▶
South Africa DD/MM/YYYY
-
USA MM/DD/YYYY
-

The dangerous one: the US writes month first. 05/06 = 6 June in SA but 5 June in the US. Read every date field on forms.

12Time format▶
→

Watch AM/PM: 12 AM = midnight, 12 PM = noon.

Say it like a local · SA → US words
ADriving & roads▶
The two famous ones: a robot is a traffic light, and a circle is a roundabout (often a traffic circle). Say "robot" to an American and they picture a machine.
Robot→Traffic light
Circle→Roundabout / traffic circle
Bakkie→Pickup (truck)
Petrol→Gas / gasoline
Garage (for fuel)→Gas station
Boot (of car)→Trunk
Bonnet→Hood
Windscreen→Windshield
Indicator→Turn signal / blinker
Hooter→Horn
Number plate→License plate
Licence disc→Registration tag / sticker
Highway→Freeway / interstate
Off-ramp→Exit
Traffic department→DMVWhere you get your license and register a car
BMoney, admin & school▶
SARS (tax)→IRS
Medical aid→Health insurance
Chemist→Pharmacy / drugstore
Postal code→ZIP code
CV→Resume
Cellphone→Cell phone / cell
Varsity→College
Matric→High school diploma
Marks→Grades
Maths→Math
CShopping & food▶
Trolley→Cart
Queue→Line
Till→Register / checkout
Cooldrink→Soda / pop
Chips (hot)→Fries
Chips (packet)→Chips
Sweets→Candy
Biscuits→Cookies
Mince→Ground beef
Takeaways→Takeout / to-go
Braai→BBQ / cookout / grill out
DHome & household▶
Flat→Apartment
Geyser→Water heater
Tap→Faucet
Lift→Elevator
Ground floor→First floorUS floor numbers are +1: SA 1st floor = US 2nd floor
Pavement→Sidewalk
Rubbish / refuse→Trash / garbage
Torch→Flashlight
Cupboard→Closet / cabinet
EHealth & emergencies▶
Casualty→ER / emergency room
Sister (nurse)→Nurse
Doctor's rooms→Doctor's office
Script→Prescription
Plaster→Band-Aid
Nappy→Diaper
Dummy→Pacifier
Emergencies: dial 911 (not 10111 or 10177). One number covers police, fire and ambulance.
FEveryday speech, be aware▶
"Now / now-now / just now"→soon / laterAmericans take "now" literally. Say a real time.
"Is it?"→"Really?" / "Oh yeah?"
"Shame"→"Aww" / "that’s sweet"In the US "shame" sounds negative, not endearing.
"Howzit"→"Hi, how’s it going?"
"Lekker"→"Great / nice / awesome"
"Robot" (again)→Traffic lightThe reflex to unlearn first.
16

Protect your family

Once income lands · cheap or free to start✓▶

Put a safety net under the family. This is the part people delay and regret. The moves below are cheap or free to start, and they matter most when there is a young child.

1Term life cover, the bridge▶

Buy affordable term life as a bridge until your income is stable. Term cover is cheap, and funeral and burial cost sits inside a term policy, so you usually do not need a separate funeral policy.

  • A child can be covered by a low-cost rider on a parent’s policy instead of a standalone policy.
  • Banks are not the channel. Many banks only refer you on, or sell through a pricier middleman. Buy direct from the insurer or through a comparison broker, it is cheaper and cleaner.
  • Heads up: a recent-immigrant flag can bump your health class or add cost. Underwriting to Standard instead of Preferred can add 20 to 35%. Quotes are a floor, not a ceiling.
Scam flag: lookalike websites harvest your details. Confirm you are on the real insurer or bank domain before entering anything.
2Beneficiaries, the free critical move▶
  • Name your spouse as primary beneficiary on each policy.
  • NEVER name a minor child directly. A payout over $15,000 to a minor is forced into court-supervised guardianship and handed over in full at 18.
  • Instead name a trusted adult as a UTMA custodian (contingent), who holds the money for the child. Free, and it keeps the money out of court until a trust is affordable.
  • If both parents are legal parents and one dies, the survivor continues as sole legal parent automatically, no court action.
  • When claiming, order several certified death certificates first, you need them for every account.
3Guardianship and paperwork, two steps▶

Now (free or low cost): set the beneficiaries above. Optionally file a state Preneed Guardian Declaration naming who would raise your child if both parents die, important if the family travels together. Defer a DIY will if you cannot meet the formalities (two witnesses plus a notary), an invalid will is worse than none.

Later (when income lands): an attorney-drafted will (guardian of the person and of the property), a revocable living trust to receive proceeds with staggered release, swap the contingent from the UTMA custodian to the trust, and ladder up cover, add to it, do not replace it.

4Keep one dossier▶

Keep a single reference document so your spouse knows what exists and what to do under stress: policies and claim numbers, the custodian’s contact, and where documents live, both physical and digital. Keep it current as you build the plan out.

17

Beyond 90 days: Year 1 to Year 5

Final time-sequenced activity · Year 1 to Year 5✓▶

The first 90 days get you safe and settled. This section is the next chapter: building credit, understanding your pay and your rights, getting on the road, the green card at year one, taxes and refunds, and the path to citizenship. Read it when each one comes up, it is reference, not a checklist.

1Credit score & financial survival▶

Your South African credit history does not transfer (Nova Credit does not cover South Africa). You start at zero, a "thin file", and your first FICO score appears about 6 months after your first credit line reports.
