What FICO is: a score from 300 to 850. Roughly: payment history ~35%, how much of your limit you use ~30%, age of your accounts ~15%, new credit ~10%, credit mix ~10%. The two you control fastest: pay every bill on time, and keep balances under 30% of the limit.

  • Secured credit card (the fastest legit builder): you put down a deposit ($200 to $500) and that becomes your limit. Put one small recurring bill on it, pay it in full every month, never go over 30%. After 6 to 12 months it often converts to a normal card and the deposit comes back.
  • Credit-builder loan: a small loan a credit union holds in a locked savings account while you pay it off; you get the money at the end. Pure payment history. Credit unions and CDFIs offer these.
  • Authorized user: a trusted person can add you to an old, well-paid card; their history can report on your file.
  • Experian Boost (free): adds utility, phone and streaming payments to your Experian file.
Avoid the debt traps. Payday loans, car-title loans, "no credit check" financing and rent-to-own carry APRs of 300% and up and are built to trap you. For emergency cash, a credit union small-dollar loan or a CDFI is the safe route.

First car loan reality: with no US file, expect a high APR (often 12 to 25%+) or a co-signer ask. Avoid "buy here, pay here" lots. Better: build a secured-card history first, then get pre-approved at a credit union (far cheaper than dealer financing) and buy a modest, reliable used car.

2Employment deep dive: pay, rights, unemployment▶

Read your pay stub. Gross pay is before deductions; net is take-home. Deductions you will see: federal income tax withholding, Social Security (6.2%) and Medicare (1.45%) = FICA, plus any health or retirement contributions. Florida has no state income tax, one less line. The YTD columns track the year. Check your hours and rate every single stub.

  • W-2 vs 1099, know which you are before you accept: W-2 = employee. The employer withholds your taxes, pays half your FICA, and you get protections. 1099 = independent contractor. Nothing is withheld, you owe self-employment tax (~15.3%) plus income tax yourself, you must set aside ~25 to 30% and pay quarterly. A 1099 role that pays more per hour can leave you with less after tax and no protections.
  • Overtime: federal law (FLSA) gives non-exempt employees 1.5x pay for hours over 40 in a week. Salaried "exempt" staff may not get it. Keep your own record of hours worked.
  • Workplace rights: you must be paid for every hour worked. Florida minimum wage rises $1 a year to $15 by 30 Sep 2026 ($14 from 30 Sep 2025). You cannot legally be underpaid because you are a refugee. OSHA protects your safety; anti-discrimination law covers national origin, race and religion. Completing an I-9 with your EAD and SSN is normal and legal. Report wage theft to the US Department of Labor Wage and Hour Division.
  • Unemployment (Reemployment Assistance): if you lose a job through no fault of your own after building US work history, you can claim state Reemployment Assistance (Florida: floridajobs.org, the CONNECT system). It is an earned benefit funded by employer taxes, not welfare, and does not affect your immigration status or green card. Apply the week the job ends and keep a weekly job-search log.
Florida employer directory400 employers, finance roles and job fairs from the supplied June 2026 job board. External URLs stay inactive.
Open job board
3Florida job board: employer directory, finance roles, job fairs▶
Source snapshot, 20 June 2026

Florida employer directory

Browse the supplied Orlando, Fort Lauderdale and Tampa job material inside the offline playbook. Employer and event URLs are intentionally shown as inactive reference text, not tappable links. Copy or type a URL into your browser only when you are ready to verify the current opening.

400employers
62finance employers
18job-fair entries
Verify before applying. Listings, dates, pay and hiring status change. Never pay to submit an application. Confirm every opening on the employer's own career site and follow current Form I-9 instructions.
Orlando Broward Finance Job fairs Salary guide
01

  3. 35

📅 JUNE 2026 (Act Now)

📍 AdventHealth Orlando Campus

601 E Rollins St, Orlando, FL 32803

4603 W Colonial Dr, Orlando, FL 32808

Former Spirit finance/IT/HR staff fast-tracked to corporate roles.

Job fair success guide 17 actions
Before The Fair
  • Pre-register online : recruiters review profiles 24-48 hrs in advance and flag candidates for private slots.
  • Research every attending employer : know their open roles, company size, and one recent news item.
  • Tailor your resume for 3-5 target employers at the fair : bring employer-specific versions.
  • Prepare a 30-second pitch: Who you are | What you do | What you're looking for.
  • Bring 10-15 printed copies of your resume, a notepad, and pen for follow-up notes.
  • Dress business professional : treat it exactly like a job interview.
At The Fair
  • Arrive early (first 30 minutes) : recruiters are freshest and queues are shortest.
  • Hit your top 5 employers first, then work through the rest.
  • Ask every recruiter: 'What does your ideal candidate look like?' : then tailor your pitch.
  • Collect a business card from every recruiter you speak with.
  • If offered an on-the-spot interview, say YES : even if you are unsure about the role.
  • Visit CareerSource booths : they can hand-deliver your resume to employers on the same day.
After The Fair
  • Email every recruiter you met within 24 hours. Reference your conversation specifically.
  • Connect on LinkedIn with a personal note mentioning the fair and what you discussed.
  • Apply online to every company you spoke with : even if they said they would follow up.
  • For CareerSource events: check in the next business day to follow up on any referrals made.
  • Track every employer you spoke with, what was discussed, and your follow-up deadline.
Florida finance salary guide and tips 12 role ranges
  • Staff Accountant Entry: $45K-$55K Mid: $55K-$70K Sr: $70K-$85K
  • Financial Analyst Entry: $55K-$65K Mid: $65K-$85K Sr: $85K-$110K
  • Senior Financial Analyst Entry: $75K-$90K Mid: $90K-$110K Sr: $110K-$130K
  • FP&A Manager Entry: $90K-$110K Mid: $110K-$135K Sr: $135K-$160K
  • Controller Entry: $90K-$120K Mid: $120K-$155K Sr: $155K-$200K
  • CFO Entry: $150K+ Mid: $200K+ Sr: $300K+
  • Investment Banking Analyst Entry: $60K-$80K Mid: $80K-$120K Sr: $120K+
  • Revenue Cycle Analyst Entry: $45K-$55K Mid: $55K-$70K Sr: $70K-$90K
  • Internal Auditor Entry: $55K-$70K Mid: $70K-$90K Sr: $90K-$120K
  • Tax Analyst Entry: $55K-$70K Mid: $70K-$90K Sr: $90K-$120K
  • Budget Analyst Entry: $50K-$65K Mid: $65K-$80K Sr: $80K-$100K
  • Actuarial Analyst Entry: $65K-$80K Mid: $80K-$100K Sr: $100K-$135K
Job seeker tips
  • Tampa is the hidden gem : Raymond James, Jabil, JPMorgan, Citi, Goldman Sachs all have major finance operations there with lower competition than NYC/Miami.
  • Government finance roles (Orange County, Broward County, City of Tampa, State of Florida) consistently post new openings : check https://jobs.myflorida.com weekly.
  • Healthcare finance (Revenue Cycle, Budget Analyst) is the fastest-growing segment : AdventHealth, BayCare, Memorial, and Broward Health all have large teams.
  • CPA license dramatically increases salary ranges and interview rates in Florida.
  • NetSuite, Workday, SAP, Oracle Financials : proficiency in any of these systems appears in 80%+ of Florida finance job postings.
  • CareerSource Tampa Bay Finance Job Fair: Amerant Bank Arena, June 25, 2026 | 10AM-2PM | FREE : Raymond James confirmed attending.
  • Former Spirit Airlines corporate finance/IT staff (Fort Lauderdale): target AutoNation, Chewy, UKG, and DHL : all FTL-area HQs currently hiring finance professionals.
  • EAD (Employment Authorization Document) + SSN is sufficient documentation for all employers listed. Form I-9 completed Day 1.
Imported from the supplied Florida Job Board, Verified Employer Directory, June 2026. The app keeps the URLs inactive by design and does not contact, track, or transmit data to any employer.
4Transportation support is under Driving▶

The full transport bridge, bus-pass support, Uber and Lyft guidance, Florida learner test route, road-test vehicle plan, BBM public business contact, and rental-company comparison now sit together under Driving and transport.

Open transport supportOpen driving learner test
5Green card (I-485), your year-1 milestone▶

As a refugee you are required to apply for your green card (adjustment of status, Form I-485) after one year of physical presence in the US. Start gathering documents around month 9 to 10.

  • No fee. Refugees pay no I-485 filing fee and no biometric fee. If anyone tells you to pay the standard fee, stop and check.
  • Medical (I-693): because you already did the overseas panel-physician medical, you usually submit only the partial I-693, the vaccination record, not a whole new exam, unless a "Class A" condition was flagged. Since 2 Dec 2024 the I-693 must be filed together with the I-485, so have the sealed vaccination record ready before you mail.
  • Documents: I-94 showing refugee admission, proof of 1 year of physical presence (lease, school or pay records), 2 passport photos, birth certificate, Form I-643, the sealed I-693 vaccination record, and police/court records only if any apply. File a separate I-485 for each family member.
  • Processing: timelines vary, often many months to over a year. You get a receipt, then a biometrics appointment, and many refugee cases are approved by mail with no interview.
  • Travel warning: your refugee I-94 says "not valid for re-entry". Do not leave the US before the green card is in hand without a Refugee Travel Document (Form I-131, fee-exempt for refugees), or you can be stranded outside. Apply well before any trip.

This is exactly what your agency's 5-year Legal Services window is for, ask CRS Legal Services to help you file.

6Taxes for new arrivals (and the refunds)▶

You file a US federal return for any year you had US income, due about 15 April of the following year. Florida has no state income tax, so usually federal only. Even if you earned little, file, the refundable credits below can be real cash back.

  • Child Tax Credit (CTC): up to $2,200 per qualifying child for the 2025 tax year, with up to $1,700 per child refundable. The child needs a work-valid SSN and you (the filer) need an SSN.
  • Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC): for low-to-moderate earned income; you, your spouse and qualifying children all need valid SSNs and your main home must be in the US over half the year.
Cross-border trap: if you claim the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (Form 2555) on SA income you become ineligible for the EITC, and filing "married separately" disqualifies the CTC from 2025. Decide this with the cross-border adviser (see the Budget tab) before you file.

File for free, do not pay for a simple return:

  • VITA (Volunteer Income Tax Assistance): free, in person, IRS-certified volunteers, for income roughly under $67,000, including limited-English speakers. Find a site with the IRS "Get Free Tax Prep" locator. Online equivalents: GetYourRefund.org and MyFreeTaxes (United Way).
  • IRS Free File: free guided software if your AGI is $89,000 or under, start only at IRS.gov/freefile (going straight to a brand site can lose the free offer). Note: the IRS Direct File pilot was discontinued for 2026.

Common mistakes: not filing because "I barely earned" (you may be owed a refund), missing the SSN-before-deadline rule for credits, paying a storefront preparer for a free-eligible return, and forgetting the cross-border duties (FBAR / FATCA, see the Budget tab).

7Year 1 to Year 5 roadmap▶
  • Year 1: build credit from zero (secured card), get your licence and a reliable car, file your first tax return and claim every credit, and file the I-485 green card at your 1-year anniversary.
  • Years 1 to 2: keep employment steady (the 5-year refugee employment services still cover you), convert credentials via DCF Refugee Services, push your FICO toward 700+, and build a 3-month emergency fund.
  • Years 2 to 3: green card in hand; travel on a Refugee Travel Document until then if needed. Start retirement saving, a workplace 401(k), especially any employer match (free money), or an IRA / Roth IRA.
  • Years 3 to 5: with good credit and a deposit, home ownership gets realistic (FHA loans allow a lower deposit). Keep retirement contributions growing.
  • Year 5: you can apply for US citizenship (naturalisation, Form N-400) 5 years after your green card's effective date, which for refugees is backdated to your arrival, so it often arrives sooner than you expect. Citizenship locks in travel freedom and the vote.
8Things nobody warns you about▶
  • Utility deposits: with no US credit, the power company and others may want a deposit ($200 to $400) to switch service on; it is credited back later once you show payment history. Ask about waiving it with proof of income.
  • Apartment application fees: most landlords charge a non-refundable application / credit-check fee ($50 to $100 per adult), on top of a security deposit and often first + last month. No US credit can mean a bigger deposit or a guarantor.
  • "Free" school is not fully free: public school is free, but budget a supply list per child ($50 to $150) plus activity, field-trip, technology and sports fees through the year. Apply for free/reduced lunch on day one.
  • Medical co-pays and surprise bills: even with Medicaid, know the cost order, clinic < urgent care < ER. The ER is the most expensive by far for non-emergencies. Ask if a provider takes your plan before the visit.
  • Credit checks everywhere: apartments, some jobs, utilities and insurance all pull credit. That is why building a score early (subtab 1) pays off fast.
  • Car insurance shock: the first premium looks absurd because you have no US driving record. It is temporary, shop around, raise the deductible, re-quote every 6 to 12 months.
9Emergency & escalation guide▶

Always start in writing and keep copies, email creates a record a phone call does not.

  • Your resettlement agency (services, money, treatment): use the written grievance process first (see the Grievance tab). If unresolved, escalate to the agency's national office, then to the funder.
  • State Refugee Coordinator: every state has one (in Florida via DCF Refugee Services). They oversee the agencies and can take complaints the local office will not resolve.
  • Federal: the Office of Refugee Resettlement (ORR) sets the rules agencies follow; serious unresolved issues can go to ORR. The DHS Office for Civil Rights and Civil Liberties (CRCL) takes civil-rights complaints.
  • Housing: landlords must follow Florida landlord-tenant law (deposits, repairs, and the legal eviction process, a landlord cannot just lock you out). Housing discrimination (national origin, family status, race, etc.) goes to HUD Fair Housing, 1-800-669-9777, free. Local legal aid handles tenant disputes.
  • Fast help: 2-1-1 connects you to local crisis, housing, food and utility help 24/7. 9-1-1 is emergencies only.
Long-term referenceBenefits, health, legal duties, storms, jobs, and EnglishThe Safety Net material now sits inside the final Year 1 to Year 5 activity.
SN

Refugee safety net: benefits, health, duties, storms

Inside activity 17, Year 1 to Year 5▶

The topics here sit outside the first-90-days flow but matter for every refugee family: which benefit track you are actually on, mental health, the legal duties that run for years, hurricane safety, and free job and English help. Several of these changed in 2025 and 2026, so confirm your own situation with your caseworker.

1Which benefit track are you on? (and SNAP + Medicaid changes)▶

Refugee support comes through a few different channels. Know which one is yours, and which ones recently shrank.

  • Matching Grant (MG): the employment-focused track run by your resettlement agency for roughly the first 8 months. Cash plus rent and utility help while you job-hunt, you take a job fast and the agency keeps supporting you while employed. Covered in the Match Grant tab.
  • Refugee Cash Assistance (RCA) + Refugee Medical Assistance (RMA): the state public-assistance track (in Florida, DCF via MyACCESS Florida), for refugees not in Matching Grant. RCA is cash; RMA is medical cover equal to Medicaid. ⚠ Both were cut to just 4 months from your eligibility date for arrivals on or after 5 May 2025 (it used to be 12). Apply early at myflfamilies.com (ACCESS). You are generally in MG or RCA/RMA, not both, ask your caseworker which fits you.
  • SNAP (food stamps): ⚠ since 1 November 2025 a 2025 federal law (OBBBA) removed SNAP eligibility for refugees until they become green-card holders. This is being challenged in court and guidance keeps shifting, so confirm with your caseworker. Meanwhile food help runs through MG / RCA and the food-bank circuit (see the Food tab).
  • Medicaid: ⚠ the same law ends Medicaid and CHIP for refugees from 1 October 2026 (cover limited mainly to green-card holders after that). That can leave a gap between Oct 2026 and when your green card (I-485, filed at your 1-year mark) is approved. Plan ahead: file the green card as early as allowed, ask about RMA and community health centers (FQHCs) that charge on a sliding scale, and watch the news, this is in flux and may shift with litigation.
Bottom line: these programs are short and changing. Treat employment plus the green card as your real safety net, and use your caseworker to map exactly what you qualify for this month.
2Mental health & adjustment▶

Resettlement is one of the most stressful things a family can go through. Struggling is normal, not weakness, and the dip often comes after the busy first weeks.

  • Free or low-cost help: your agency can refer you to refugee-specific, trauma-informed counseling (often with interpreters). Community health centers (FQHCs) offer low-cost mental health care regardless of insurance.
  • In a crisis: call or text 988 (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline, 24/7, free, interpreters available), or 911 for immediate danger.
  • For children: school counselors are free, tell the school the family is newly arrived so they can help your child settle.
  • What helps day to day: sleep, routine, small wins, time outdoors, and staying connected to community (see the job-help tab for groups).
  • Worth a professional conversation, early: persistent sadness, sleeplessness, withdrawal, or a sharp change in a child's behaviour. Reaching out early is a strength.
3Ongoing legal duties (do not let these lapse)▶
  • Change of address, Form AR-11: you must tell USCIS within 10 days of every move, file it free online at uscis.gov, for each family member. It is a legal duty for non-citizens.
  • Selective Service (males 18 to 25): almost all males in the US aged 18 to 25, refugees included, must register at sss.gov (free, a few minutes). It is tied to future citizenship and federal aid, do it within 30 days of arriving or turning 18.
  • EAD work-permit renewal: your first work permit (EAD) is time-limited. If your green card has not arrived yet, renew it (Form I-765) well before it expires, a lapse can stop you working. The green card later replaces the EAD.
  • Keep your USCIS account and documents current, and keep copies of everything (see the Documents tab).
4Hurricane & disaster prep (Florida)▶

Florida's hurricane season runs 1 June to 30 November. New arrivals get caught out, a little prep keeps you safe.

  • Know your evacuation zone (search your county + "evacuation zone") and how you would leave without a car if you had to.
  • Get alerts: sign up for your county's emergency alerts and the free FEMA app; watch local news once a storm is named.
  • Basic kit: a few days of water (1 gallon per person per day) and non-perishable food, medicines, a power bank and chargers, a flashlight, copies of your documents in a waterproof bag, and some cash (cards and ATMs fail in outages).
  • Renters: renters' insurance does not cover flood. Know your building's plan and where the shelters are (county sites, some are pet-friendly).
  • When told to evacuate, go early, roads jam fast. Official info: ready.gov and your county emergency-management site.
5Free job help & English classes▶

Beyond your agency, free national and local services help refugees get working and learn English.

  • Upwardly Global (upwardlyglobal.org): free and national, helps work-authorized immigrants and refugees restart professional careers, CV, interview prep, and employer connections. Strong for degree-holders.
  • Local resettlement & charities: Catholic Charities, the International Rescue Committee (IRC) and your CRS office run free job-readiness and placement programs, ask even if you did not arrive through them.
  • American Job Centers / CareerSource (Florida): free government career centers, job listings, resume help, and sometimes funded training. Many have a refugee program.
  • Free English (ESL): public libraries, adult-education programs, and refugee/community organisations run free ESL classes, in person and online. Ask your library first, it is the easiest door.
  • Pair these with the credential-conversion funding (see the 5-year safety-net tab) so your prior qualifications count here.
Browse the Florida job boardUse the employer directory with CareerSource, resume help and English classes. URLs are reference text only.
Open job board
★

Green card to citizen: PR, family, and staying legal

Reference · the whole point of year 1, and the path to year 5▶

This is the arc the move is for: refugee now, green card (permanent residence) at year 1, US citizen around year 5. Plus how to bring immediate family, how to protect your status until you naturalize, and your South African consular contacts.

Your tracked actions · 0 of 5 actions ticked. Tap a box to tick it. Your ticks are saved on this device. Tip: use Add to Home Screen so your ticks stay saved permanently.
  • ✓ File I-485 green card. Start gathering documents around month 9 to 10 of year 1.
  • ✓ File I-730 to bring spouse or under-21 children. Hard deadline: within 2 years of arrival.
  • ✓ File N-400 for citizenship at year 5. You can file up to 90 days early.
  • ✓ Save your nearest SA consulate (DIRCO) contact for passport and document help.
  • ✓ Confirm each filing with CRS Legal Services, your free 5-year legal window.
1Green card (PR), your year-1 milestone▶

As a refugee you are required to apply for your green card (lawful permanent residence, "PR") after one year of physical presence. This is Form I-485, adjustment of status under INA section 209. It is the central milestone of your first year. Start gathering documents around month 9 to 10.

  • No fee. Refugees pay no I-485 filing fee and no biometric fee. If anyone asks you to pay the standard fee, stop and check.
  • Backdated to your arrival, this is the big one. When approved, your green card is dated to the day you arrived. So your 5-year clock toward citizenship runs from arrival, not from when the card prints.
  • Medical: usually only the partial I-693 (vaccination record), not a new exam, filed together with the I-485.
  • Refugees use a dedicated path (section 209). Recent guidance about leaving the US to get a green card is aimed at other visa categories (section 245), not refugees. The rules are tightening, so confirm the current procedure with your CRS Legal Services before filing.
  • Documents: I-94 refugee admission, proof of 1 year of physical presence (lease, school or pay records), 2 photos, birth certificate, Form I-643, the sealed I-693 vaccination record, and police or court records only if any apply. A separate I-485 for each family member.
  • Do not travel outside the US before the green card is in hand without a Refugee Travel Document (Form I-131, fee-exempt), or you can be stranded.

Use your agency's free CRS Legal Services (the 5-year legal-services window) to file.

2The path to citizenship (N-400)▶

Citizenship is the end goal. It locks in your status for good, gives you a US passport and the vote, and removes any exposure to future policy changes.

  • When: apply for naturalization (Form N-400) after 5 years as a green-card holder, and because your green card is backdated to arrival, the 5 years count from when you arrived. You can file up to 90 days early.
  • Requirements: 5 years as a permanent resident with continuous residence, physical presence in the US about 30 months of those 5 years, good moral character, basic English (read, write, speak), pass the US civics test, and be 18 or older.
  • The process: file N-400, give biometrics, attend an interview (English and civics test), then take the Oath of Allegiance.
  • Children: kids who hold a green card and are under 18 often become citizens automatically when a parent naturalizes (derivative citizenship). Confirm with legal services.
  • Protect the clock: good moral character is judged across the whole period. The red flags in the next tab can reset or block it.
3Bring your family (I-730, the 2-year window)▶

If you left immediate family behind, you may bring them through "following-to-join" using Form I-730 (Refugee/Asylee Relative Petition).

  • Who qualifies: your legally recognized spouse, and your unmarried children under 21, who were your spouse or child at the time you were admitted as a refugee.
  • Hard deadline: file within 2 years of your arrival. Do not let this window close.
  • They get your status: approved relatives receive the same derivative refugee status, and in most cases there is no filing fee and no financial sponsorship requirement.
  • Process: file an I-730 for each relative, USCIS reviews, then overseas processing brings them to the US.

File early through CRS Legal Services. The 2-year clock is unforgiving.

4Stay safe legally (deportation-risk red flags)▶

Some things that are minor or legal for citizens can threaten your status as a non-citizen. Until you naturalize, protect your record.

  • Do NOT vote until you are a US citizen. Registering or voting as a non-citizen is a serious immigration offence and can end your path to citizenship. You vote once you naturalize.
  • Firearms: gun laws vary by state and some non-citizens are barred. Do not buy or possess a firearm without confirming your eligibility with a lawyer first.
  • Criminal charges: even minor convictions (theft, fraud, drugs, domestic violence, DUI) can carry immigration consequences including removal. If you are ever arrested or charged, get an immigration lawyer, not just a criminal one, before you plead to anything.
  • Honesty with USCIS: never lie or use false documents on any form. Misrepresentation is a permanent bar.
  • Social media: USCIS may review your online activity when they adjudicate. Keep it lawful and consistent with your applications.

Free or low-cost immigration legal help is available through your resettlement agency and nonprofit legal aid. When in doubt, ask before you act.

5SA embassy and consulates (DIRCO)▶

For a South African passport renewal, SA birth or marriage documents, or notarial help while in the US, contact your nearest South African mission.

  • Embassy of South Africa, Washington DC: dirco.gov.za/washington
  • Consulate General, New York: dirco.gov.za/newyork
  • Consulate General, Los Angeles: dirco.gov.za/losangeles

There is no SA consulate in Florida; these three missions cover the whole US. Use them for SA document requests, not for US immigration questions (that is your CRS Legal Services).

★

Refugee alphabet: abbreviations and acronyms

Reference · what all these letters actually mean▶

The resettlement world runs on acronyms. Here is what the common ones mean. Rules and benefits change, so always confirm the current detail with your resettlement agency.

1Abbreviations and acronyms guide▶
AcronymMeaning
USRAPUnited States Refugee Admissions Program
PIRProgram for Initial Resettlement
PCPreferred Communities
MGMatching Grant
RCARefugee Cash Assistance
RMARefugee Medical Assistance
SSISupplemental Security Income
SNAPSupplemental Nutrition Assistance Program
EADEmployment Authorization Document
SSNSocial Security Number
RTDRefugee Travel Document
ORROffice of Refugee Resettlement
PRMBureau of Population, Refugees and Migration
VOLAGVoluntary Agency
IRCInternational Rescue Committee
CWSChurch World Service
LIRSLutheran Immigration and Refugee Service
USCISU.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services
I-730Refugee/Asylee Relative Petition
I-485Application to Register Permanent Residence (Green Card)
I-693Report of Immigration Medical Examination
N-400Application for Naturalization (U.S. Citizenship)
2Most common terms, explained▶
  • PIR, Program for Initial Resettlement. Funding used during the first resettlement period to help refugees establish themselves in the United States.
  • PC, Preferred Communities. Additional support for refugees with higher needs or vulnerabilities.
  • MG, Matching Grant. Employment-focused programme designed to help refugees become self-sufficient.
  • RCA, Refugee Cash Assistance. Temporary cash assistance for eligible refugees.
  • RMA, Refugee Medical Assistance. Medical assistance for eligible refugees.
  • SSI, Supplemental Security Income. Federal benefit for qualifying elderly, blind, or disabled individuals.
  • I-730, Refugee/Asylee Relative Petition. Allows certain family members of refugees and asylees to join them in the U.S.
  • I-485, Application for a Green Card. Application to become a Lawful Permanent Resident.
  • RTD, Refugee Travel Document. Travel document used by refugees for international travel.
FINAL v31 interaction and sync release: complete FINAL content, the professional four-tab app shell, ultra realistic 3D tab visuals, the embedded 59 page Florida learner cheat sheet, the driving learner test hub, visible transport support under Driving, the food map, local tools, road-test rental guidance, and the offline Florida employer directory with intentionally inactive external URLs. Quality review source: FINAL v31 handoff, 21 June 2026.
Built by a family that landed before you. Share it forward.
This playbook is a community guideline drawn from previous families' experiences, not advice, every case differs, verify everything against your own documents.
SARAH · South African Refugee Assistance & Help groups on Facebook · 9-1-1 emergencies · 2-1-1 local help, 24/7.
No names, no case numbers in this file by design. Your details stay yours.
Local and private

Profile and setup

This is not an identity profile. It only controls optional estimates, milestones, display preferences, and data stored on this device.

No personal profile is created. The app does not ask for a name, email, home address, case number, document number, or banking information.
Profile inputs update Stats immediately and save locally when browser storage is available.
